description: The starting point of the C++ course: program structure, fundamental types and variables, references, control flow, functions and basic stream I/O. Two decks, easy to harder; every card is answerable from the primer.
primer:
## What this set covers
The absolute foundations of C++: how a program is built, the type system, and the two building blocks of logic — control flow and functions. Skip it if you already write C++; otherwise every card here traces back to this page.

## Program structure & I/O
- C++ is **statically & strongly typed and compiled** to native machine code. Execution starts at `int main()`; returning `0` means success (falling off the end of `main` implicitly returns `0`).
- `#include <header>` is a **preprocessor** directive that pastes a header in before compilation (`<...>` = system paths, `"..."` = project first).
- Output: `std::cout << x;` uses the **insertion** operator `<<` (chains left-to-right). Input: `std::cin >> x;` uses **extraction** `>>`. Prefer `'\n'` over `std::endl` — `endl` also **flushes** (slow in loops).
- A **namespace** scopes names; the standard library lives in `std`, so `std::cout`. `using namespace std;` imports it all (avoid in headers).

## Types & variables
- Integers: `int`, `short`, `long`, `long long` (+`unsigned` variants), `char`, `bool`. Floating: `float`, `double`, `long double`. Sizes are implementation-defined (`int` usually 32-bit, `double` 64-bit); `sizeof(T)` gives the byte size.
- Initialise with **braces**: `int x{42};` — braces **forbid narrowing** (`int x{3.14}` is an error). `int x = 42;` and `int x(42);` also work. An **uninitialised local has an indeterminate value**; reading it is undefined behaviour, so always initialise (`int n{};` zero-initialises).
- `const` = read-only after initialisation (may be set from a run-time value). `constexpr` = a **compile-time** constant, usable where the language demands one.
- `auto` **deduces** a variable's type from its initialiser (`auto i = 0;` is `int`) — still a single static type.
- A **reference** `int& r = x;` is an **alias** for an existing object: must be initialised, never rebindable, never null.
- `'a'` is a single `char`; `"a"` is a 2-char array (letter + trailing `'\0'`).

## Arithmetic gotchas
- With two `int` operands, `/` is **integer division** (truncates toward zero) and `%` is the remainder: `5/2` is `2`, `5%2` is `1`. Force floating-point by making an operand a `double` (e.g. `/ 2.0`).
- All-int sub-expressions are computed in integers *before* any widening: `(3+4)/2` is `3`, not `3.5`.
- `unsigned` can never be negative; decrementing `0` **wraps** to the maximum value, so `for (unsigned i; ...; --i) i >= 0` loops forever.

## Control flow
- Loops: `for` (explicit counter), `while` (test first, may run 0×), `do...while` (runs once then tests), and the **range-based for** `for (const auto& x : v)` (use `&` to modify/avoid copying).
- `switch` **falls through** each `case` unless you `break`. `break` exits the nearest loop/switch; `continue` skips to the next iteration.
- The **ternary** `cond ? a : b` is an expression form of if/else.

## Functions
- A **declaration** (prototype) gives the signature and ends with `;`; a **definition** adds the body `{...}`. Declare many times, define once (One Definition Rule).
- **Pass by value** copies the argument (good for small/cheap types); **pass by reference** `T&` aliases the caller's object (to mutate it or avoid copying). Use **`const T&`** for read-only parameters that are expensive to copy.
- **Default arguments** (`int pow(int b, int e = 2);`) must be trailing. **Overloading** = same name, different parameter lists (return type alone can't distinguish). `void` returns nothing.
- A variable's **scope** is the block where it is visible/alive (destroyed at the closing brace). **Recursion** needs a base case + a step toward it. **Never return a reference/pointer to a local** — it dangles when the function returns.
